content.text: A pooling strategy is the operation that reduces a sequence of token-level hidden states produced by a Transformer encoder into a single fixed-size vector representing the entire sentence or document. The Transformer encoder operates on all tokens in parallel and outputs one hidden state per token. For a sentence of N tokens with a model of dimension d, the encoder produces a matrix of shape (N, d). Pooling collapses this matrix along the sequence dimension to produce a single vector in R^d that can be stored, indexed, and compared with other sentence vectors.

The choice of pooling strategy has a substantial effect on downstream task quality. Different pooling methods emphasize different aspects of the token representations: CLS pooling relies on the special [CLS] token that was trained to aggregate sequence-level information; mean pooling treats all content tokens equally; max pooling captures the strongest signal along each dimension regardless of which token produced it; and attention-weighted pooling learns to emphasize semantically important tokens dynamically. No single strategy is universally best — the optimal choice depends on the model architecture, the training objective, and the downstream task.

Understanding pooling strategies requires understanding the preceding encoder. A BERT-style model's [CLS] token has been trained via NSP (Next Sentence Prediction) to carry sentence-level information, making CLS pooling natural for classification tasks. But for sentence similarity tasks with contrastive training objectives as in sentence-transformers, mean pooling consistently outperforms CLS pooling because the contrastive loss distributes similarity information across all token positions rather than concentrating it in [CLS].

Pooling is the bottleneck between a rich position-aware token representation and a single vector that must represent the entire semantic content. The wrong pooling choice can silently degrade retrieval quality by 5 to 15 MTEB points, a difference equivalent to switching between a small and large model.

In production retrieval systems, mismatched pooling is one of the most common and costly silent bugs. Consider a team deploying BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5 with CLS pooling instead of mean pooling. This model was fine-tuned with mean pooling as the aggregation strategy; using CLS pooling at inference time produces embeddings from a token representation that was never trained to carry sentence-level meaning. Recall@10 on their internal dataset dropped from 79% to 61%, a gap that took weeks to diagnose because the pipeline produced no errors.

Pooling strategy is also a performance consideration. CLS pooling is O(1) in the pooling step, requiring only indexing the first token position. Mean and max pooling are O(N) with very small constants. Attention pooling adds a learned linear layer but enables dynamic weighting. For high-throughput embedding servers processing millions of queries, the constant factors matter even when the asymptotic complexity is the same.

attrs.alt: Pooling strategies: CLS, mean, max, and attention pooling applied to token hidden states to produce a sentence embedding

content.text: Benefits of Choosing the Right Strategy
Mean pooling on contrastively-trained models (sentence-transformers, E5, BGE) consistently outperforms CLS pooling by 3-8 MTEB points on retrieval tasks.
CLS pooling is better for classification tasks where the model was pre-trained with NSP; mean pooling is better for similarity tasks trained with contrastive loss.
CLS pooling requires no additional computation beyond indexing; mean pooling adds one masked average; attention pooling adds a learned linear layer but enables dynamic token weighting.
Attention-mask-aware mean pooling correctly ignores padding tokens, preventing their zero-valued representations from diluting the embedding.
Pooling is a post-hoc operation on frozen encoder outputs; you can experiment with different strategies without re-running the full encoder forward pass.
Works well only when the model was explicitly trained with a [CLS]-level objective such as BERT's NSP. Performs poorly on RoBERTa or contrastively fine-tuned models.
Stopwords like 'the', 'a', and 'of' contribute equally to the final embedding. For very long documents, the relevant semantic content is diluted by high-frequency but low-information tokens.
Taking the maximum over each dimension discards information about which tokens co-occurred. Two sentences with the same vocabulary but opposite meanings can produce similar max-pooled vectors.
Requires learning an attention vector, adding a small amount of extra computation and a parameter that must be trained or fine-tuned.
For decoder-only GPT-style models, the last non-padding token's hidden state is used as the sequence embedding. Applying this to BERT-style models is incorrect and produces poor embeddings.

A transformer encoder like BERT outputs one vector per input token. For a sentence with 5 tokens, that means 5 vectors each of dimension 768. But downstream tasks — semantic search, clustering, classification — need a
representing the whole sentence. Pooling is the operation that collapses those token vectors into one.
The choice of pooling strategy has a large effect on downstream quality. The wrong strategy can cut benchmark performance nearly in half.

attrs.alt: Four pooling strategies: [CLS] token, mean pooling (SBERT default), max pooling, and weighted mean — with STS benchmark Spearman ρ comparison bars

content.text: token and its final hidden state is designed (via Next Sentence Prediction pre-training) to summarize the whole sequence. Taking this vector directly is the simplest possible pooling strategy.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

sentence = "The cat sat on the mat"
inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# [CLS] is always position 0
cls_embedding = outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]  # shape: (1, 768)
print(cls_embedding.shape)  # torch.Size([1, 768])
The problem: BERT's NSP objective was designed for sentence-pair classification, not for producing geometrically meaningful sentence vectors. Without fine-tuning for semantic similarity,
vectors produce very poor cosine similarities — the STS-B Spearman ρ is around 0.30, barely above chance.
Average the token embeddings for all real (non-padding) tokens, typically excluding
indicating which positions are real tokens:
and consistently outperforms other strategies without fine-tuning. The attention mask division handles variable-length sequences and padding correctly.

def mean_pool(token_embeddings, attention_mask):
    """Mask-aware mean pooling over real tokens."""
    # Expand mask to match embedding dimension
    mask_expanded = attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1).float()  # (B, T, 1)
    # Zero out padding token embeddings
    summed = (token_embeddings * mask_expanded).sum(dim=1)  # (B, 768)
    # Divide by actual token count per sample
    count = mask_expanded.sum(dim=1).clamp(min=1e-9)       # (B, 1)
    return summed / count


sentences = ["The cat sat", "A dog ran quickly through the park"]
inputs = tokenizer(sentences, return_tensors="pt", padding=True, truncation=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

embeddings = mean_pool(outputs.last_hidden_state, inputs["attention_mask"])
print(embeddings.shape)  # (2, 768)
Take the element-wise maximum across all token positions for each embedding dimension:

Max pooling captures the strongest feature activation in each dimension. Intuitively, if any token strongly signals a feature (e.g. dimension 42 encodes "financial context"), the max preserves that signal even if only one token triggers it. This makes it useful for classification tasks where the presence of a feature matters more than its average intensity.

def max_pool(token_embeddings, attention_mask):
    """Element-wise max over real (non-padded) token positions."""
    # Replace padding positions with a very large negative number
    # so they don't win the max
    mask_expanded = attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1).float()
    token_embeddings[mask_expanded == 0] = -1e9
    return token_embeddings.max(dim=1).values  # (B, 768)

embeddings = max_pool(outputs.last_hidden_state.clone(), inputs["attention_mask"])
print(embeddings.shape)  # (2, 768)
for each token position and form a weighted average:
is a learned parameter. The model can learn to up-weight content words and down-weight function words or punctuation. Some SBERT variants (e.g. models with a
module) extend this across transformer layers rather than across positions.

import torch.nn as nn

class AttentionPooling(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden_size: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.attention = nn.Linear(hidden_size, 1)

    def forward(self, token_embeddings, attention_mask):
        # Score each token
        scores = self.attention(token_embeddings).squeeze(-1)  # (B, T)
        # Mask padding before softmax
        scores = scores.masked_fill(attention_mask == 0, -1e9)
        weights = scores.softmax(dim=-1).unsqueeze(-1)         # (B, T, 1)
        return (token_embeddings * weights).sum(dim=1)          # (B, 768)

pooler = AttentionPooling(hidden_size=768)
embeddings = pooler(outputs.last_hidden_state, inputs["attention_mask"])
print(embeddings.shape)  # (2, 768)
Fine-tuned NSP tasks; avoid for similarity without FT
Semantic similarity, search, clustering — strong default
When token importance is asymmetric (e.g. topic sentences)
All semantic similarity tasks — best overall

Fine-tuning matters far more than pooling strategy. SBERT with mean pooling and fine-tuning (ρ ≈ 0.85) beats every pooling strategy applied to a frozen BERT (best: ρ ≈ 0.60). Pooling selection is relevant when you cannot fine-tune — otherwise prioritize training data and loss function.
library handles pooling for you via a
module that is composed with the transformer backbone:

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, models

# Inspect the pooling module of a pre-built model
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
for name, module in model.named_modules():
    if "Pooling" in type(module).__name__:
        print(module.get_config_dict())
# {'word_embedding_dimension': 384,
#  'pooling_mode_cls_token': False,
#  'pooling_mode_mean_tokens': True,    <-- mean pooling is active
#  'pooling_mode_max_tokens': False, ...}

# Build a custom model with max pooling instead
transformer = models.Transformer("bert-base-uncased")
pooling = models.Pooling(
    transformer.get_word_embedding_dimension(),
    pooling_mode_mean_tokens=False,
    pooling_mode_max_tokens=True,
)
custom_model = SentenceTransformer(modules=[transformer, pooling])
Pooling across layers (rather than across tokens) is a separate but related idea. Instead of using only the last hidden layer, you can combine multiple layers:

inputs = tokenizer("The cat sat", return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs, output_hidden_states=True)

# outputs.hidden_states: tuple of (n_layers + 1) tensors of shape (1, T, 768)
# Concatenate last 4 layers then mean-pool across tokens
last_4 = torch.stack(outputs.hidden_states[-4:], dim=0)  # (4, 1, T, 768)
mean_layers = last_4.mean(dim=0)                          # (1, T, 768)
sentence_vec = mean_layers.mean(dim=1)                    # (1, 768)

# Alternative: sum last 4 layers (common in NER tasks)
sum_layers = last_4.sum(dim=0).mean(dim=1)               # (1, 768)

Layer combination is most relevant when you extract embeddings from a frozen model (no fine-tuning). Earlier layers carry more syntactic information; later layers are more task-specific. For semantic similarity with fine-tuning, the last layer with mean pooling is typically sufficient.
Use mean pooling. It requires no extra parameters, is fast, and generalizes well across tasks.
without fine-tuning — the NSP pre-training objective does not produce comparable sentence vectors.
Any pooling strategy on a fine-tuned SBERT model will outperform the best pooling on a frozen model.
Always apply the attention mask when pooling. Including padding token embeddings in the mean/max distorts the result, especially for short sentences in a batch.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")

sentences = [
    "Pooling converts token representations to a sentence vector.",
    "The cat sat on the mat.",
]

encoded = tokenizer(sentences, padding=True, truncation=True,
                    max_length=512, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**encoded)

hidden_states = outputs.last_hidden_state  # (batch, seq_len, hidden_dim)
attention_mask = encoded["attention_mask"]  # (batch, seq_len)


# CLS Pooling
cls_embeddings = hidden_states[:, 0, :]  # (batch, hidden_dim)
cls_norm = F.normalize(cls_embeddings, p=2, dim=-1)


# Mean Pooling (mask-aware)
mask_expanded = attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1).float()  # (batch, seq_len, 1)
sum_hidden = (hidden_states * mask_expanded).sum(dim=1)  # (batch, hidden_dim)
token_counts = mask_expanded.sum(dim=1).clamp(min=1e-9)  # (batch, 1)
mean_embeddings = sum_hidden / token_counts
mean_norm = F.normalize(mean_embeddings, p=2, dim=-1)


# Max Pooling (mask-aware)
mask_bool = attention_mask.bool().unsqueeze(-1).expand_as(hidden_states)
masked = hidden_states.masked_fill(~mask_bool, float('-inf'))
max_embeddings = masked.max(dim=1).values  # (batch, hidden_dim)
max_norm = F.normalize(max_embeddings, p=2, dim=-1)


# Attention Pooling (learned)
class AttentionPooling(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.attention = torch.nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)

    def forward(self, hidden_states, attention_mask):
        scores = self.attention(hidden_states).squeeze(-1)  # (batch, seq_len)
        scores = scores.masked_fill(attention_mask == 0, float('-inf'))
        weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)  # (batch, seq_len)
        pooled = (weights.unsqueeze(-1) * hidden_states).sum(dim=1)
        return F.normalize(pooled, p=2, dim=-1)

attn_pool = AttentionPooling(hidden_states.shape[-1])
with torch.no_grad():
    attn_embeddings = attn_pool(hidden_states, attention_mask)

def cosine_sim(a, b): return (a * b).sum(dim=-1)

for name, emb in [("CLS", cls_norm), ("Mean", mean_norm), ("Max", max_norm)]:
    sim = cosine_sim(emb[0:1], emb[1:2]).item()
    print(f"{name} pooling cosine similarity: {sim:.4f}")
Real-World Example: Retrieval Quality by Pooling Strategy

In a production document retrieval system over 500K legal documents, switching from CLS to mean pooling on sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 improved Recall@10 from 68% to 79%. The model was fine-tuned with mean pooling; using CLS pooling at inference time was an implementation mistake caught only during ablation experiments.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

# sentence-transformers reads pooling config from modules.json automatically
model = SentenceTransformer("sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2")
# Default: mean pooling + L2 normalize

corpus = [
    "The court held that the defendant breached the duty of care.",
    "Plaintiff was awarded compensatory damages for negligent misrepresentation.",
    "Force majeure clauses excuse performance when extraordinary events occur.",
]
query = "What damages are available for negligent misrepresentation?"

corpus_embs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True)
query_emb = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)

scores = (query_emb @ corpus_embs.T)[0]
ranked = np.argsort(-scores)
for rank, idx in enumerate(ranked):
    print(f"Rank {rank+1} (score={scores[idx]:.3f}): {corpus[idx][:70]}...")
Using CLS pooling on sentence-transformers models:
These models are trained with mean pooling; CLS pooling yields significantly lower retrieval quality. Always check the model's modules.json to confirm the expected pooling type.
Ignoring attention mask in mean pooling:
Computing hidden_states.mean(dim=1) without masking includes padding tokens, diluting the result. Always use mask-weighted mean.
Using max pooling for semantic similarity:
Max pooling is appropriate for multi-label classification where feature presence matters; it consistently underperforms mean pooling for similarity on retrieval benchmarks.
dot_product(a, b) does not equal cosine_similarity(a, b) unless both vectors are unit-normalized. Always L2-normalize before storing embeddings if you plan to use dot product for retrieval.
Applying last-token pooling to BERT models:
Last-token pooling is for causal decoder models such as GPT; it produces near-random embeddings when applied to bidirectional encoders.
The following BEIR MSMARCO retrieval NDCG@10 scores for all-mpnet-base-v2 illustrate the practical performance difference between pooling strategies:
The standard library that pools and normalizes automatically per model config. Use model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True) and pooling is handled correctly.
For custom pooling use AutoModel plus manual pooling code as shown above. Required when integrating into non-standard pipelines.
The inference server handles pooling internally via the model's pooling config; specify with --pooling mean or --pooling cls.
Stores L2-normalized embeddings; use IndexFlatIP (inner product) for cosine similarity search, which equals cosine for unit vectors.

The pooling strategy is part of your retrieval index schema. Changing pooling invalidates all stored embeddings. Treat pooling config as immutable for a given index version and enforce this in deployment pipelines.
Padding token leakage in naive mean:
Naive mean without masking slightly biases embeddings toward zero; for very short queries relative to the max_length, padding constitutes most of the sequence and the distortion is material.

Pooling is deterministic given the same token hidden states. Embedding reproducibility failures usually trace to tokenization differences such as different max_length or truncation, not pooling itself.
Pooling reduces a (N, d) token matrix from the Transformer encoder to a single (d,) sentence vector; the choice of strategy significantly affects downstream quality.
Mean pooling with attention-mask awareness is the best general-purpose strategy for models trained with contrastive objectives such as sentence-transformers, E5, and BGE.
CLS pooling is appropriate only for models explicitly trained with a [CLS]-level objective like BERT with NSP; it underperforms mean pooling on retrieval tasks for other models.
Max pooling captures peak feature values but discards co-occurrence information; it is better suited to multi-label classification than semantic similarity tasks.
Attention pooling adds a learned scalar weight per token enabling the model to emphasize important tokens; marginal gains over mean pooling rarely justify the added complexity.
Always L2-normalize after pooling so that dot product equals cosine similarity, enabling efficient inner product search in vector databases.
Mismatched pooling such as CLS on a mean-pooling-trained model is one of the most common and silent production bugs; validate with paraphrase similarity sanity checks after deployment.
For documents longer than 512 tokens, embed each chunk independently and optionally pool the chunk embeddings to create a document-level representation.
